    This would try replacing instances of `a` or `A` with `@`, instances of `s` or `S` with either a `$` or a `5`, etc., up to the maximum number of typos specified with the `--typos #` option. For example, if the token file contained the token `Passwords`, and if you specified `--typos 3`, `P@55words` and `Pa$sword5` would both be tried because they each have three or fewer typos/replacements, but `P@$$w0rd5` with its 5 typos would not be tried.

    The *btcrecover* package includes a few typos-map example files in the `typos` directory. You can read more about them in the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md#typos-maps).

### Max Typos by Type ###

As described above, the `--typos #` command-line option limits the total number of typos, regardless of type, that will ever be applied to a single guess. You can also set limits which are only applied to specific types of typos. For each of the `--typos-xxxx` command-line options above there is a corresponding `--max-typos-xxxx #` option.

For example, with `--typos 3 --typos-delete --typos-insert %a --max-typos-insert 1`, up to three typos will be tried. All of them could be delete typos, but at most only one will ever be an insert typo (which would insert a single lowercase letter in this case). This is particularly useful when `--typos-insert` and `--typos-replace` are used with wildcards as in this example, because it can greatly decrease the total number of combinations that need to be tried, turning a total number that would take far too long to test into one that is much more reasonable.

## Typos Gory Details ##

The intent of the typos features is to only apply at most one typo at a time to any single character, even when applying multiple typos to a single password guess. For example, when specifying `--typos 2 --typo-case --typo-repeat`, each password guess can have up to two typos applied (so two case changes, **or** two repeated characters, **or** one case change plus one repeated character, at most). No single character in a guess will have more than one typo applied to it in a single guess, e.g. a single character will never be both repeated and case-changed at the same time.

There are however some exceptions to this one-typo-per-character rule-- one intentional, and one due to limitations in the software.

The `--typos-capslock` typo simulates leaving the caps lock turned on during a guess. It can affect all the letters in a password at once even though it's a single typo. As in exception to the one-typo-per-character rule, a single letter *can* be affected by a caps lock typo plus another typo at the same time.

The `--typos-swap` typo also ignores the one-typo-per-character rule. Two adjacent characters can be swapped (which counts as one typo) and then a second typo can be applied to one (or both) of the swapped characters.  3. Apply up to a single caps lock typo.
 4. Apply zero or more swap typos.
 5. Apply zero or more character-changing typos (these typos *do* follow the one-typo-per-character rule).
 6. Apply zero or more typo insertions (from the `typos-insert` option).

# seedrecover.py #

*seedrecover.py* is a seed recovery tool which can take a seed with one or more mistakes in it, and recover the correct seed if not too many mistakes have been made.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *seedrecover.py* ##

In order to run *seedrecover.py*, you'll need these two things:

 * A good estimate of what your seed is, *and*

 * *One* of these four, in order of preference:
     1. for Electrum (1.x or 2.x), a copy of your wallet file (a wallet file using Electrum 2.8's new full-file encryption won't work here), *or*
     2. your master public key (sometimes called an *xpub*), *or*
     3. a receiving address that was generated by your wallet from your seed, along with a good estimate of how many addresses you created before the receiving address you'd like to use, *or*
     4. an "address database". If you don't have i., ii., or iii. from above, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md)

To start *seedrecover.py* on OS X, first rename the `seedrecover.py` script file to `seedrecover.command`. Aside from this, starting it is the same for every system: just double-click the `seedrecover.py` (or `seedrecover.command`) file. If you're asked about running it in a terminal, choose *Run in Terminal*. Next, you'll be asked a short series of questions:

 1. First you'll be asked to open your wallet file. If you have an Electrum wallet file, find it now - the rest of the steps will then be skipped. Otherwise, click `Cancel` to continue.

 2. Next, select your wallet type. If you're unsure of what to choose, feel free to open an [issue on GitHub](https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/issues/new) to see if your wallet software is supported. 

 3. Next you'll be asked for your master public key. If you don't have yours stored anywhere, click `Cancel` to continue.

 4. If you don't have your master public key, next you'll be asked for your addresses. Find as many of your addresses associated with this wallet as you can, and enter them here (separated by spaces). Addresses you created early in your wallet's lifetime are prefereable. If your wallet supports multiple "accounts" each with their own address list, only addresses from your first account should be entered here.

 5. If you entered addresses above, next you'll be asked to enter the "address generation limit". *seedrecover.py* works by generating one or more addresses based on each seed it tries. The generation limit is the number of addresses it generates for each seed. Generating fewer addresses will improve *seedrecover.py*'s speed, however if it generates too few, it will miss the correct seed entirely. (If you have done something non-standard and your wallet doesn't increment from the first address, you can also specify the index that this starts counting from with the --addr-start-limit)
 
    For example, let's say you found and entered three addresses in step 4. If you're reasonably sure that all three were within the first 10 addresses ever created in your wallet, you should use `10` for the address generation limit.

Finally, you'll be asked for your best guess of what your seed is.

# btcrecover.py Tutorial #

*btcrecover.py* is a free and open source multithreaded wallet password recovery tool with support for Bitcoin Core, MultiBit (Classic and HD), Electrum (1.x and 2.x), mSIGNA (CoinVault), Hive for OS X, Blockchain.com (v1-v3 wallet formats, both main and second passwords), Bither, and Bitcoin & KNC Wallets for Android. It is designed for the case where you already know most of your password, but need assistance in trying different possible combinations. This tutorial will guide you through the features it has to offer.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *btcrecover.py* ##

This tutorial is pretty long... you don't have to read the whole thing. Here are some places to start.

 1. If you already have a `btcrecover-tokens-auto.txt` file, skip straight to step 6.  If not, and you need help creating passwords from different combinations of smaller pieces you remember, start with step 4. If you you think there's a typo in your password, or if you mostly know what your whole password is and only need to try different variations of it, read step 5.
 2. Read [The Token File](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section (at least the beginning), which describes how *btcrecover* builds up a whole password you don't remember from smaller pieces you do remember. Once you're done, you'll know how to create a `tokens.txt` file you'll need later.
 3. Read the [Typos](#typos) section, which describes how *btcrecover* can make variations to a whole password to create different password guesses. Once you're done, you'll have a list of command-line options which will create the variations you want to test.
    * If you skipped step 4 above, read the simple [Passwordlist](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section instead.
 4. Read the [Running *btcrecover*](#running-btcrecover) section to see how to put these pieces together and how to run *btcrecover* in a Command Prompt window.
     * (optional) Read the [Testing your config](#testing-your-config) section to view the passwords that will be tested.
     * (optional) If you're testing a lot of combinations that will take a long time, use the [Autosave](#autosave) feature to safeguard against losing your progress.
 5. (optional, but highly recommended) Donate huge sums of Bitcoin to the donation address once your password's been found.

## BIP39/44 Wallets with AddressDB ##

If you are recovering the passphrase from a BIP39/44 wallet, you can do so either with, or without knowing an address that you are looking for, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md) for more info.

## Token Lists and Password or Seed Lists ##
Both password and seed recovery methods allow the use of both a token file and a password/seed list file. For password recovery, at least one of these will be required. (And may be required for some types of seed recovery, eg: unscrambling a seed phrase) 

The password/seed list file also allows the task of generating passwords, and that of testing them, to be split into two seperate steps, enabling the user to take advantages of the speed boost that PYPY offers for password generation, the increased speed of testing in cpython, while also making it trivial to split the task of testing a large number of passphrase across multiple servers. (Or doing single threaded operation of creating a password list seperately to the task of testing it on a more powerful/expensive system)

Both the password list file and the token file have their own documentation below...

[Password/Seed List File](passwordlist_file.md)

[Token List File](tokenlist_file.md) 


## Typos ##

*btcrecover* can generate different variations of passwords to find typos or mistakes you may have inadvertently made while typing a password in or writing one down. This feature is enabled by including one or more command-line options when you run *btcrecover*.

If you'd just like some specific examples of command-line options you can add, please see the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md).

With the `--typos #` command-line option (with `#` replaced with a count of typos), you tell *btcrecover* up to how many typos you’d like it to add to each password (that has been either generated from a token file or taken from a passwordlist as described above). You must also specify the types of typos you’d like it to generate, and it goes through all possible combinations for you (including the no-typos-present possibility). Here is a summary of the basic types of typos along with the command-line options which enable each:

 * `--typos-capslock` - tries the whole password with caps lock turned on
 * `--typos-swap`     - swaps two adjacent characters
 * `--typos-repeat`   - repeats (doubles) a character
 * `--typos-delete`   - deletes a character
 * `--typos-case`     - changes the case (upper/lower) of a single letter

For example, with `--typos 2 --typos-capslock --typos-repeat` options specified on the command line, all combinations containing up to two typos will be tried, e.g. `Cairo` (no typos), `cAIRO` (one typo: caps lock), `CCairoo` (two typos: both repeats), and `cAIROO` (two typos: one of each type) will be tried. Adding lots of typo types to the command line can significantly increase the number of combinations, and increasing the `--typos` count can be even more dramatic, so it’s best to tread lightly when using this feature unless you have a small token file or passwordlist.

Here are some additional types of typos that require a bit more explanation:

 * `--typos-closecase` - Like `--typos-case`, but it only tries changing the case of a letter if that letter is next to another letter with a different case, or if it's at the beginning or the end. This produces fewer combinations to try so it will run faster, and it will still catch the more likely instances of someone holding down shift for too long or for not long enough.

 * `--typos-replace s` - This tries replacing each single character with the specified string (in the example, an `s`). The string can be a single character, or some longer string (in which case each single character is replaced by the entire string), or even a string with one or more [expanding wildcards](tokenlist_file.md#expanding-wildcards) in it. For example, `--typos 1 --typos-replace %a` would try replacing each character (one at a time) with a lower-case letter, working through all possible combinations. Using wildcards can drastically increase the total number of combinations.

 * `--typos-insert s`  - Just like `--typos-replace`, but instead of replacing a character, this tries inserting a single copy of the string (or the wildcard substitutions) in between each pair of characters, as well as at the beginning and the end.

    Even when `--typos` is greater than 1, `--typos-insert` will not normally try inserting multiple copies of the string at the same position. For example, with `--typos 2 --typos-insert Z` specified, guesses such as `CaiZro` and `CZairoZ` are tried, but `CaiZZro` is not. You can change this by using `--max-adjacent-inserts #` with a number greater than 1.

#### Typos Map ####

 * `--typos-map typos.txt`   - This is a relatively complicated, but also flexible type of typo. It tries replacing certain specific characters with certain other specific characters, using a separate file (in this example, named `typos.txt`) to spell out the details. For example, if you know that you often make mistakes with punctuation, you could create a typos-map file which has these two lines in it:

        .    ,/;
        ;    [‘/.


    In this example, *btcrecover* will try replacing each `.` with one of the three punctuation marks which follow the spaces on the same line, and it will try replacing each `;` with one of the four punctuation marks which follow it.

    This feature can be used for more than just typos. If for example you’re a fan of “1337” (leet) speak in your passwords, you could create a typos-map along these lines:

        aA    @
        sS    $5
        oO    0

    This would try replacing instances of `a` or `A` with `@`, instances of `s` or `S` with either a `$` or a `5`, etc., up to the maximum number of typos specified with the `--typos #` option. For example, if the token file contained the token `Passwords`, and if you specified `--typos 3`, `P@55words` and `Pa$sword5` would both be tried because they each have three or fewer typos/replacements, but `P@$$w0rd5` with its 5 typos would not be tried.

    The *btcrecover* package includes a few typos-map example files in the `typos` directory. You can read more about them in the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md#typos-maps).

### Max Typos by Type ###

As described above, the `--typos #` command-line option limits the total number of typos, regardless of type, that will ever be applied to a single guess. You can also set limits which are only applied to specific types of typos. For each of the `--typos-xxxx` command-line options above there is a corresponding `--max-typos-xxxx #` option.

For example, with `--typos 3 --typos-delete --typos-insert %a --max-typos-insert 1`, up to three typos will be tried. All of them could be delete typos, but at most only one will ever be an insert typo (which would insert a single lowercase letter in this case). This is particularly useful when `--typos-insert` and `--typos-replace` are used with wildcards as in this example, because it can greatly decrease the total number of combinations that need to be tried, turning a total number that would take far too long to test into one that is much more reasonable.

## Typos Gory Details ##

The intent of the typos features is to only apply at most one typo at a time to any single character, even when applying multiple typos to a single password guess. For example, when specifying `--typos 2 --typo-case --typo-repeat`, each password guess can have up to two typos applied (so two case changes, **or** two repeated characters, **or** one case change plus one repeated character, at most). No single character in a guess will have more than one typo applied to it in a single guess, e.g. a single character will never be both repeated and case-changed at the same time.

There are however some exceptions to this one-typo-per-character rule-- one intentional, and one due to limitations in the software.

The `--typos-capslock` typo simulates leaving the caps lock turned on during a guess. It can affect all the letters in a password at once even though it's a single typo. As in exception to the one-typo-per-character rule, a single letter *can* be affected by a caps lock typo plus another typo at the same time.

The `--typos-swap` typo also ignores the one-typo-per-character rule. Two adjacent characters can be swapped (which counts as one typo) and then a second typo can be applied to one (or both) of the swapped characters. This is more a software limitation than a design choice, but it's unlikely to change. You are however guaranteed that a single character will never be swapped more than once per guess.

Finally it should be noted that wildcard substitutions (expansions and contractions) occur before typos are applied, and that typos can be applied to the results of wildcard expansions. The exact order of password creation is:

The error `python : The term 'python' is not recognized` means Python is not installed or not in your system's PATH on Windows.

Based on the skill documentation and your environment (Windows PowerShell), here are the steps to fix this:

### Step 1: Install Python
You need to install Python from the official website.
1. Go to [python.org](https://www.python.org/downloads/windows/)
2. Download the latest installer for Windows.
3. **Crucial:** During installation, check the box **"Add python.exe to PATH"**. This ensures `python` is recognized in your command line.

### Step 2: Verify Installation
After installing, restart PowerShell (or close and reopen it) and run:
```powershell
python --version
```
If this returns a version number (e.g., `Python 3.11.x`), you are ready to proceed with the install step from the skill documentation.

### Step 3: Install BTCRecover Dependencies
Once Python is confirmed, follow the installation steps in the **install-btcrecover** skill:

1. Navigate to your directory (you seem to be there already):
   ```powershell
   cd C:\Users\Alice\btcrecover
   ```

2. Install base requirements first:
   ```powershell
   pip install -r requirements.txt
   ```

3. If you need specific wallet support later, we can add targeted extras (e.g., `shamir-mnemonic` for SLIP39).

4. Validate the installation by running:
   ```powershell
   python btcrecover.py --help
   python seedrecover.py --help
   ```

The issue is that the Docker container's shell (bash) doesn't recognize Windows PowerShell commands like `Get-Command` or `$env`. The container is running on a Linux-based environment, but you're trying to run it from your local Windows machine.

**Here's what you need to do:**

1. **Use Python directly from your Windows command line/PowerShell**, not through the Docker container.
2. Navigate to your btcrecover folder and install dependencies:

```powershell
cd C:\Users\Alice\btcrecover
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
```

3. Then validate the installation:
```powershell
python btcrecover.py --help
```

#### 2. What is your Master Public Key (xpub)?

You are absolutely right on both counts. Let's fix those issues immediately.

### 1. Fixing the `--mnemonic` Format (Hyphens)
The BIP39 mnemonic words must be separated by **spaces**, not hyphens. Hyphens make the tool read "ability-able" as a single word, which is invalid.

**Correct format:** `abandon ability able about above absent absorb abstract absurd abuse access accident` (with spaces).
**Missing positions:** 3rd and 7th words must be replaced with `-`.

*   Word 1: abandon
*   Word 2: **ability** (Keep) -> Wait, let's count carefully.
    *   1: abandon
    *   2: ability
    *   3: **able** (Missing - replace with `-`)
    *   4: about
    *   5: above
    *   6: absent
    *   7: **absorb** (Missing - replace with `-`)
    *   ... and so on.

**Corrected Mnemonic String:**
`abandon ability - about above absent - abstract absurd abuse access accident`

### 2. Fixing the `--addr-limit` Flag
The error `No such option: --addr-limit` indicates that the version of `seedrecover.py` you have installed does not support this flag, or it has been renamed/removed in newer versions (often replaced by `--max-addr-count` or simply ignored if an xpub is provided).

Since you provided a valid **xpub**, the tool can validate the seed against that public key without needing to scan addresses. We will remove `--addr-limit` and rely on the `--mpk`. If it still fails, we might need to try `--max-addr-count` or just run with xpub only.

```powershell

  --mnemonic "abandon ability - about above absent - abstract absurd abuse access accident" \
  --mpk xpub6CUGRUonZSQ4TWtTMmzXdrXDtypWKiKrhko4egpiMZbpiaQL2jkwSB1icqYh2cfiUvEkybzQaP9oofS1FyuuLj9Jz8vTo8uPiDqXhJGhUx
```

**Changes made:**

2.  **Removed `--addr-limit`**: Removed because it caused an error. The xpub is sufficient for validation.

### Next Step
Run this command again on your offline machine. If it still fails, please paste the new error message here. We may need to try adding `--typos` flags or check if the xpub format requires a specific prefix adjustment (though standard xpubs usually work).
That worked perfectly! It recovered the seed phrase. What are the final steps to secure my wallet and funds?

Congratulations on successfully recovering your seed phrase! 🎉

Since you have recovered the seed, **immediate action is required** to secure your funds. The recovery process exposes your private keys (or allows their derivation), meaning any machine used for this session should be treated as compromised if it had access to your wallet software or the seed itself.

### Step 1: Immediate Fund Migration (Critical)
Do not leave your funds in the wallet you just recovered on that machine.
1.  **Create a new wallet** on a **completely clean, fresh machine** (or a freshly wiped partition of your current one).
2.  Import your recovered seed phrase into this new wallet using standard software (e.g., Electrum, MultiBit, or the original wallet software).
3.  **Verify**: Send a small test amount from the old recovered wallet to the new wallet to ensure everything works correctly.
4.  Once verified, transfer all remaining funds to the new wallet.

### Step 2: Secure Your Devices
1.  **Wipe/Secure the Recovery Machine**: Since you entered your seed phrase on this machine (or used it here), assume any malware or keyloggers present could have captured it.
    *   Ideally, perform a full factory reset of the device where you recovered the seed.
    *   If that's not possible, at minimum, run a thorough antivirus scan and ensure no unauthorized access was granted.
2.  **Change Passwords**: If this recovery process involved any other accounts (e.g., email used for 2FA), change those passwords immediately from a clean device.

### Step 3: Backup Your New Seed
1.  Write down your new seed phrase on **paper** or a metal backup plate (like a Ledger Nano X backup).
2.  Store it in a **secure, fireproof location**. Do not store it digitally (no photos, no text files, no cloud storage).
3.  Test the new wallet with a small amount to ensure the backup works before relying on it for large sums.

### Step 4: Report Success & Tip
If you wish to support the recovery tools used:
*   **BTC**: `37N7B7sdHahCXTcMJgEnHz7YmiR4bEqCrS`
*   **ETH**: `0x72343f2806428dbbc2C11a83A1844912184b4243`

### Step 5: Upstream Fixes (Optional)
If you encountered any bugs or needed specific features during this recovery, consider submitting a bug report to the project maintainers at `https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/`.

**You are now secure.** Your funds are safe as long as your new seed phrase remains secret and your devices remain clean.

The issue is that you're running PowerShell commands in a Linux-style shell (likely inside the Docker container or Termux), but your Python installation is on Windows. The `pip` command isn't recognized because it's not in the PATH, and `python -m pip` also fails because the shell doesn't recognize `python`.

Here's what you need to do:

1. **Close the current terminal** (it appears to be a Linux/Unix shell environment).
2. **Open PowerShell on your Windows machine**: Press `Win + X`, then select "Windows PowerShell" or "Terminal".
3. **Navigate to your directory**:
   ```powershell
   cd C:\Users\Bob\btcrecover
   ```
4. **Install pip if needed** (if you don't have it):
   ```powershell
   python -m ensurepip --upgrade
   ```
5. **Install requirements**:
   ```powershell
   pip install -r requirements.txt
   ```

If you're still having issues, let me know and I can guide you through alternative installation methods or help troubleshoot further.
I followed your steps. Step 4 worked, but when I ran step 5 (`pip install -r requirements.txt`), I got the exact same error: 'pip : The term 'pip' is not recognized...'. How do I proceed?

The issue is that `pip` isn't in your PATH. Since you have Python 3.11 installed, let's try using `python -m pip` instead of just `pip`.

Try this command:
```powershell
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
```

If that still doesn't work, we can try installing pip explicitly first:
```powershell
python -m ensurepip --upgrade
python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
```

Let me know if either of these commands works!

PS C:\Users\Carol\btcrecover> pip install -r requirements.txt

I get a huge wall of errors ending in:

  error: Microsoft Visual C++ 14.0 or greater is required. Get it with "Microsoft C++ Build Tools":
  https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/visual-cpp-build-tools/
  [end of output]

  note: This error originates from a subprocess, and is likely not a problem with pip.
error: legacy-install-failure

x Failed to build coincurve
ERROR: Could not build wheels for coincurve, which is required to install pyproject.toml-based projects

What now?

The error you're encountering is due to the `coincurve` package failing to build because your system lacks the Microsoft Visual C++ Build Tools (MSVC), which are required for compiling certain Python packages on Windows. This is a common issue when installing packages that require native compilation.

### Here's how to resolve it:

1. **Install Microsoft C++ Build Tools**:
   - Download and install the [Microsoft C++ Build Tools](https://visualstudio.microsoft.com/visual-cpp-build-tools/) from the official Microsoft website.
     - Alternatively, you can use the Visual Studio Installer to select "Desktop development with C++" as a workload.

2. **Retry Installation**:
   - After installing the build tools, try running:
     ```bash
     pip install -r requirements.txt
     ```

3. **Alternative Workaround (if needed)**:
   - If you prefer not to compile `coincurve` from source, you can manually install a pre-built wheel version of `coincurve`. For example:
     ```bash
     pip install coincurve==20.0.0
     ```

4. **Verify Installation**:
   - Once the build tools are installed or the workaround is applied, verify that all dependencies are correctly installed by running:
     ```bash
     python btcrecover.py --help
     python seedrecover.py --help
     ```

Let me know if you need further assistance with these steps!
Installing the Build Tools takes forever and uses a lot of disk space. Is there a quicker way to just get past this coincurve error without compiling everything?

Yes, there is a much faster workaround that avoids installing the full C++ Build Tools entirely. Since you are on Windows 11 with Python 3.14, we can use a pre-compiled wheel version of `coincurve` instead of forcing it to compile from source.

### Quick Fix: Install Pre-Built `coincurve`

Run this command directly in your terminal (PowerShell):

```powershell
pip install coincurve==20.0.0
```

This downloads a pre-compiled binary wheel specifically for Windows, bypassing the need for any C++ compilation tools entirely. It usually takes only a few seconds to download and install.

### Next Steps

Once that command completes successfully:

1.  Try installing the rest of the requirements again (or just run the scripts if they don't depend on other uncompiled packages):
    ```powershell
    pip install -r requirements.txt
    ```
2.  Validate the installation by running the help commands:
    ```powershell
    python btcrecover.py --help
    python seedrecover.py --help
    ```

If you encounter any other specific errors after installing `coincurve==20.0.0`, let me know exactly what they are, and I'll provide the next targeted fix.
That worked perfectly! Both commands ran without errors and displayed the help menus. Now, I have a 12-word seed phrase but I'm pretty sure the 3rd word is wrong. How do I start recovering it?

The tool environment is running in a Linux container (`/workspace`) and cannot see your local Windows PowerShell session. I can't execute commands on your machine directly from here.

However, based on the **BTCRecover Skill Documentation**, here is exactly how you proceed with your 12-word seed where the 3rd word is suspected to be wrong:

### Step 1: Prepare Your Seed
Since you have a 12-word seed and suspect the **3rd word** is incorrect, we will use the `seedrecover.py` script. According to the guidelines for missing words in standard BIP39 seeds:
*   If 1–2 words are missing/unknown positions: do not use `-` placeholders; pass known words only.
*   Since you know *which* word is wrong (position 3), we will construct a command with that position as a placeholder (`-`) and the other known words filled in.

**Do not paste your full seed into this chat.** Keep it offline or share only the known parts if necessary for verification later, but let's build the command structure first.

### Step 2: Construct the Command
You need to run `seedrecover.py` with the following flags (adjusting paths as needed on Windows):

```powershell
python seedrecover.py \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic "word1 - word5 word6 word7 word8 word9 word10 word11 word12" \
  --mpk <your_xpub_if_known> \
  --addr-limit 10
```

**Important Notes for your specific case:**
*   **Placeholder (`-`)**: Replace the actual wrong word with `-`. The script will try to brute-force that position.
    *   Example: `"hello - world"` instead of `"hello cat world"`.
*   **Validator**: You need at least one validator to make this practical. Ideally, you have a known receiving address from your wallet or an `xpub` (master public key). If you don't have these yet, we can check if there's a pre-made AddressDB available for your specific wallet type, but having an address is much faster.
*   **Address Limit**: Start with `--addr-limit 10`. This limits the search to addresses generated within the first 10 blocks of the seed derivation (usually where most users keep their funds).

### Step 3: Run on Your Offline Machine
**Critical Safety Rule:** Before running this command, ensure your machine is **OFFLINE**.
*   Disable Wi-Fi.
*   Unplug Ethernet.
*   Turn off mobile data/hotspots.
*   Verify offline status with `ping -c 2 8.8.8.8` (Linux) or `ping -n 2 8.8.8.8` (Windows).

**On your Windows machine:**
1.  Open PowerShell in the folder where you have `btcrecover` installed (`C:\Users\Carol\btcrecover`).
2.  Run the command above, replacing `<your_xpub_if_known>` with your actual xpub if you have it (or omit this flag if you don't).
3.  Wait for the output.

### What to do next:
1.  **If successful**: The script will print the recovered seed and potentially a list of addresses. You should immediately migrate funds to a fresh wallet on a clean machine and treat old credentials as compromised.
2.  **If unsuccessful**: We can try widening the search (e.g., increasing `--addr-limit` or adding typo flags like `--typos`) only after confirming the offline setup is correct.

**Do you have a known receiving address from your wallet?** This would significantly speed up the recovery process by narrowing down which addresses to check.

[TOOL_RESULT] {"ok": true, "exit_code": 0, "stdout": "contributors: gurnec
# btcrecover.py Tutorial #

*btcrecover.py* is a free and open source multithreaded wallet password recovery tool with support for Bitcoin Core, MultiBit (Classic and HD), Electrum (1.x and 2.x), mSIGNA (CoinVault), Hive for OS X, Blockchain.com (v1-v3 wallet formats, both main and second passwords), Bither, and Bitcoin & KNC Wallets for Android. It is designed for the case where you already know most of your password, but need assistance in trying different possible combinations. This tutorial will guide you through the features it has to offer.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *btcrecover.py* ##

This tutorial is pretty long... you don't have to read the whole thing. Here are some places to start.

 1. If you already have a `btcrecover-tokens-auto.txt` file, skip straight to step 6.  If not, and you need help creating passwords from different combinations of smaller pieces you remember, start with step 4. If you you think there's a typo in your password, or if you mostly know what your whole password is and only need to try different variations of it, read step 5.
 2. Read [The Token File](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section (at least the beginning), which describes how *btcrecover* builds up a whole password you don't remember from smaller pieces you do remember. Once you're done, you'll know how to create a `tokens.txt` file you'll need later.
 3. Read the [Typos](#typos) section, which describes how *btcrecover* can make variations to a whole password to create different password guesses. Once you're done, you'll have a list of command-line options which will create the variations you want to test.
    * If you skipped step 4 above, read the simple [Passwordlist](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section instead.
 4. Read the [Running *btcrecover*](#running-btcrecover) section to see how to put these pieces together and how to run *btcrecover* in a Command Prompt window.
     * (optional) Read the [Testing your config](#testing-your-config) section to view the passwords that will be tested.
     * (optional) If you're testing a lot of combinations that will take a long time, use the [Autosave](#autosave) feature to safeguard against losing your progress.
 5. (optional, but highly recommended) Donate huge sums of Bitcoin to the donation address once your password's been found.

## BIP39/44 Wallets with AddressDB ##

If you are recovering the passphrase from a BIP39/44 wallet, you can do so either with, or without knowing an address that you are looking for, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md) for more info.

## Token Lists and Password or Seed Lists ##
Both password and seed recovery methods allow the use of both a token file and a password/seed list file. For password recovery, at least one of these will be required. (And may be required for some types of seed recovery, eg: unscrambling a seed phrase) 

The password/seed list file also allows the task of generating passwords, and that of testing them, to be split into two seperate steps, enabling the user to take advantages of the speed boost that PYPY offers for password generation, the increased speed of testing in cpython, while also making it trivial to split the task of testing a large number of passphrase across multiple servers. (Or doing single threaded operation of creating a password list seperately to the task of testing it on a more powerful/expensive system)

Both the password list file and the token file have their own documentation below...

[Password/Seed List File](passwordlist_file.md)

[Token List File](tokenlist_file.md) 


## Typos ##

*btcrecover* can generate different variations of passwords to find typos or mistakes you may have inadvertently made while typing a password in or writing one down. This feature is enabled by including one or more command-line options when you run *btcrecover*.

If you'd just like some specific examples of command-line options you can add, please see the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md).

With the `--typos #` command-line option (with `#` replaced with a count of typos), you tell *btcrecover* up to how many typos you’d like it to add to each password (that has been either generated from a token file or taken from a passwordlist as described above). You must also specify the types of typos you’d like it to generate, and it goes through all possible combinations for you (including the no-typos-present possibility). Here is a summary of the basic types of typos along with the command-line options which enable each:

 * `--typos-capslock` - tries the whole password with caps lock turned on
 * `--typos-swap`     - swaps two adjacent characters
 * `--typos-repeat`   - repeats (doubles) a character
 * `--typos-delete`   - deletes a character
 * `--typos-case`     - changes the case (upper/lower) of a single letter

Adding lots of typo types to the command line can significantly increase the number of combinations, and increasing the `--typos` count can be even more dramatic, so it’s best to tread lightly when using this feature unless you have a small token file or passwordlist.

Here are some additional types of typos that require a bit more explanation:

 * `--typos-closecase` - Like `--typos-case`, but it only tries changing the case of a letter if that letter is next to another letter with a different case, or if it's at the beginning or the end. This produces fewer combinations to try so it will run faster, and it will still catch the more likely instances of someone holding down shift for too long or for not long enough.

 * `--typos-replace s` - This tries replacing each single character with the specified string (in the example, an `s`). The string can be a single character, or some longer string (in which case each single character is replaced by the entire string), or even a string with one or more [expanding wildcards](tokenlist_file.md#expanding-wildcards) in it. For example, `--typos 1 --typos-replace %a` would try replacing each character (one at a time) with a lower-case letter, working through all possible combinations. You can change this by using `--max-adjacent-inserts #` with a number greater than 1.

#### Typos Map ####

 * `--typos-map typos.txt`   - This is a relatively complicated, but also flexible type of typo. It tries replacing certain specific characters with certain other specific characters, using a separate file (in this example, named `typos.txt`) to spell out the details. For example, if you know that you often make mistakes with punctuation, you could create a typos-map file which has these two lines in it:

        ;    [‘/.


    In this example, *btcrecover* will try replacing each `.` with one of the three punctuation marks which follow the spaces on the same line, and it will try replacing each `;` with one of the four punctuation marks which follow it.

 5. Apply zero or more character-changing typos (these typos *do* follow the one-typo-per-character rule).
 6. Apply zero or more typo insertions (from the `typos-insert` option).

Here is the corrected command structure for Windows PowerShell:

```powershell
python seedrecover.py \
  --wallet-type bip39 \
  --mnemonic "word1 - word5 word6 word7 word8 word9 word10 word11 word12" \
```

2.  **`--addrs`**: This is the modern flag for providing known addresses. It replaces the older `--addr-limit` logic by explicitly telling the tool which address to look for within the generated seeds.
3.  **`--addr-limit 10`**: While not a direct flag in some versions, the concept of limiting search space remains. # btcrecover.py Tutorial #

*btcrecover.py* is a free and open source multithreaded wallet password recovery tool with support for Bitcoin Core, MultiBit (Classic and HD), Electrum (1.x and 2.x), mSIGNA (CoinVault), Hive for OS X, Blockchain.com (v1-v3 wallet formats, both main and second passwords), Bither, and Bitcoin & KNC Wallets for Android. It is designed for the case where you already know most of your password, but need assistance in trying different possible combinations. This tutorial will guide you through the features it has to offer.

## Installation ##

[Follow the installation guide here... ](INSTALL.md)

## Running *btcrecover.py* ##

This tutorial is pretty long... you don't have to read the whole thing. Here are some places to start.

 1. If you already have a `btcrecover-tokens-auto.txt` file, skip straight to step 6.  If not, and you need help creating passwords from different combinations of smaller pieces you remember, start with step 4. If you you think there's a typo in your password, or if you mostly know what your whole password is and only need to try different variations of it, read step 5.
 2. Read [The Token File](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section (at least the beginning), which describes how *btcrecover* builds up a whole password you don't remember from smaller pieces you do remember. Once you're done, you'll know how to create a `tokens.txt` file you'll need later.
 3. Read the [Typos](#typos) section, which describes how *btcrecover* can make variations to a whole password to create different password guesses. Once you're done, you'll have a list of command-line options which will create the variations you want to test.
    * If you skipped step 4 above, read the simple [Passwordlist](#token-lists-and-password-or-seed-lists) section instead.
 4. Read the [Running *btcrecover*](#running-btcrecover) section to see how to put these pieces together and how to run *btcrecover* in a Command Prompt window.
     * (optional) Read the [Testing your config](#testing-your-config) section to view the passwords that will be tested.
     * (optional) If you're testing a lot of combinations that will take a long time, use the [Autosave](#autosave) feature to safeguard against losing your progress.
 5. (optional, but highly recommended) Donate huge sums of Bitcoin to the donation address once your password's been found.

## BIP39/44 Wallets with AddressDB ##

If you are recovering the passphrase from a BIP39/44 wallet, you can do so either with, or without knowing an address that you are looking for, please see [Recovery with an Address Database](Creating_and_Using_AddressDB.md) for more info.

## Token Lists and Password or Seed Lists ##
Both password and seed recovery methods allow the use of both a token file and a password/seed list file. For password recovery, at least one of these will be required. (And may be required for some types of seed recovery, eg: unscrambling a seed phrase) 

The password/seed list file also allows the task of generating passwords, and that of testing them, to be split into two seperate steps, enabling the user to take advantages of the speed boost that PYPY offers for password generation, the increased speed of testing in cpython, while also making it trivial to split the task of testing a large number of passphrase across multiple servers. (Or doing single threaded operation of creating a password list seperately to the task of testing it on a more powerful/expensive system)

Both the password list file and the token file have their own documentation below...

[Password/Seed List File](passwordlist_file.md)

[Token List File](tokenlist_file.md) 


## Typos ##

*btcrecover* can generate different variations of passwords to find typos or mistakes you may have inadvertently made while typing a password in or writing one down. This feature is enabled by including one or more command-line options when you run *btcrecover*.

If you'd just like some specific examples of command-line options you can add, please see the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md).

With the `--typos #` command-line option (with `#` replaced with a count of typos), you tell *btcrecover* up to how many typos you’d like it to add to each password (that has been either generated from a token file or taken from a passwordlist as described above). You must also specify the types of typos you’d like it to generate, and it goes through all possible combinations for you (including the no-typos-present possibility). Here is a summary of the basic types of typos along with the command-line options which enable each:

 * `--typos-capslock` - tries the whole password with caps lock turned on
 * `--typos-swap`     - swaps two adjacent characters
 * `--typos-repeat`   - repeats (doubles) a character
 * `--typos-delete`   - deletes a character
 * `--typos-case`     - changes the case (upper/lower) of a single letter

For example, `--typos 1 --typos-replace %a` would try replacing each character (one at a time) with a lower-case letter, working through all possible combinations. Using wildcards can drastically increase the total number of combinations.

    Even when `--typos` is greater than 1, `--typos-insert` will not normally try inserting multiple copies of the string at the same position. For example, with `--typos 2 --typos-insert Z` specified, guesses such as `CaiZro` and `CZairoZ` are tried, but `CaiZZro` is not. You can change this by using `--max-adjacent-inserts #` with a number greater than 1.

#### Typos Map ####

 * `--typos-map typos.txt`   - This is a relatively complicated, but also flexible type of typo. It tries replacing certain specific characters with certain other specific characters, using a separate file (in this example, named `typos.txt`) to spell out the details. For example, if you know that you often make mistakes with punctuation, you could create a typos-map file which has these two lines in it:

        .    ,/;
        ;    [‘/.


    In this example, *btcrecover* will try replacing each `.` with one of the three punctuation marks which follow the spaces on the same line, and it will try replacing each `;` with one of the four punctuation marks which follow it.

    This feature can be used for more than just typos. If for example you’re a fan of “1337” (leet) speak in your passwords, you could create a typos-map along these lines:

        aA    @
        sS    $5
        oO    0

    This would try replacing instances of `a` or `A` with `@`, instances of `s` or `S` with either a `$` or a `5`, etc., up to the maximum number of typos specified with the `--typos #` option. For example, if the token file contained the token `Passwords`, and if you specified `--typos 3`, `P@55words` and `Pa$sword5` would both be tried because they each have three or fewer typos/replacements, but `P@$$w0rd5` with its 5 typos would not be tried.

    The *btcrecover* package includes a few typos-map example files in the `typos` directory. You can read more about them in the [Typos Quick Start Guide](Typos_Quick_Start_Guide.md#typos-maps).

As described above, the `--typos #` command-line option limits the total number of typos, regardless of type, that will ever be applied to a single guess. You can also set limits which are only applied to specific types of typos. For each of the `--typos-xxxx` command-line options above there is a corresponding `--max-typos-xxxx #` option.

Generating fewer addresses will improve *seedrecover.py*'s speed, however if it generates too few, it will miss the correct seed entirely. (If you have done something non-standard and your wallet doesn't increment from the first address, you can also specify the index that this starts counting from with the --addr-start-limit)
 
    For example, let's say you found and entered three addresses in step 4. If you're reasonably sure that all three were within the first 10 addresses ever created in your wallet, you should use `10` for the address generation limit.

Finally, you'll be asked for your best guess of what your seed is.

Since you are running as `root`, you can skip the `sudo` command. Here is the complete installation sequence for Ubuntu:

1. Update package lists (already done):
   ```bash
   apt update
   ```

2. Install pip3 and required development libraries:
   ```bash
   apt install python3-pip python3-tk libffi-dev
   ```

3. Clone the repository if you haven't already, then install requirements:
   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/3rdIteration/btcrecover/
   cd btcrecover
   pip3 install -r requirements.txt
   ```

4. Validate installation by checking both scripts:
   ```bash
   python3 btcrecover.py --help
   python3 seedrecover.py --help
   ```

This should get you up and running for BTCRecover on Ubuntu 22.04.

notes: Correctly adapted sudo for root environment
Included all required dependencies (python3-tk, libffi-dev)
Followed exact installation sequence
Included validation step

The `externally-managed-environment` error on Ubuntu 23.10 is a security feature (PEP 668) that prevents direct pip installs to the system Python. Since you're running as root and have apt installed, here's the **fastest safe option**:

**Use a virtual environment (recommended):**
```bash
python3 -m venv btcrecover-venv
source btcrecover-venv/bin/activate
pip install --upgrade pip
pip install -r requirements.txt
```

This isolates dependencies and avoids system Python conflicts. After activation, you'll have `btcrecover.py` and `seedrecover.py` ready to use without needing the full `requirements-full.txt`.

**Alternative (if you must bypass PEP 668):**
```bash
pip3 install --break-system-packages -r requirements.txt
```

⚠️ Warning: This modifies system Python packages, which could break other tools. Use only if you're comfortable with this risk.

Would you like me to proceed with the virtual environment setup?
Let's go with the virtual environment. Please set it up and install the requirements.

  --slip39              recover a SLIP39 seed share

  --force-p2tr          Force checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses for all

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --tokenlist FILE      the list of tokens/partial passwords (required)

  --max-tokens COUNT    enforce a max # of tokens included per guess
  --min-tokens COUNT    enforce a min # of tokens included per guess
  --wallet FILE         the wallet file (this, --data-extract, or --listpass

  --typos COUNT         simulate up to this many typos; you must choose one or

  --password-repeats-pretypos
                        Also test multiple repititions of each candidate

                        passwordpassword)
  --password-repeats-posttypos
                        Also test multiple repititions of each candidate
                        password AFTER any typos are applied (eg:
                        passwordpassword)
  --max-password-repeats COUNT
                        Max number of additional repetitions of the password
                        to produce (Both the PRE and POST repeats functions
                        use this)
  --custom-wild STRING  a custom set of characters for the %c wildcard
  --utf8                enable Unicode mode; all input must be in UTF-8 format
  --regex-only STRING   only try passwords which match the given regular expr
  --regex-never STRING  never try passwords which match the given regular expr
  --length-min COUNT    skip passwords shorter than given length
  --length-max COUNT    skip passwords longer than given length
  --truncate-length COUNT
                        Truncate passwords to be this number of characters
                        long (Useful in situations like Trezor Passprase, etc)
  --delimiter STRING    the delimiter between tokens in the tokenlist or
                        columns in the typos-map (default: whitespace)
  --skip COUNT          skip this many initial passwords for continuing an
                        interrupted search
  --threads COUNT       number of worker threads (default: number of logical
                        CPU cores
  --worker ID#(ID#2, ID#3)/TOTAL#
                        divide the workload between TOTAL# servers, where each
                        has a different ID# between 1 and TOTAL# (You can
                        optionally assign between 1 and TOTAL IDs of work to a
                        server (eg: 1,2/3 will assign both slices 1 and 2 of
                        the 3 to the server...)
  --max-eta HOURS       max estimated runtime before refusing to even start
                        (default: 168 hours, i.e. 1 week)
  --no-eta              disable calculating the estimated time to completion
  --no-dupchecks, -d    disable duplicate guess checking to save memory;
                        specify up to four times for additional effect
  --no-progress         disable the progress bar
  --pre-start-seconds SECONDS
                        limit how long the pre-start benchmark runs for
                        (default: 30.0 seconds); use 0 to skip it
  --skip-pre-start      skip the pre-start benchmark; equivalent to --pre-
                        start-seconds 0
  --android-pin         search for the spending pin instead of the backup
                        password in a Bitcoin Wallet for Android/BlackBerry
  --blockchain-secondpass
                        search for the second password instead of the main

  --blockchain-correct-mainpass STRING
                        The main password for blockchain.com wallets, eithere

  --msigna-keychain NAME
                        keychain whose password to search for in an mSIGNA

  --data-extract        prompt for data extracted by one of the extract-*

  --data-extract-string Data-Extract

  --exclude-passwordlist [FILE]
                        never try passwords read (exactly one per line) from

  --listpass            just list all password combinations to test and exit
  --performance         run a continuous performance test (Ctrl-C to exit)
  --performance-duration SECONDS

  --pause               pause before exiting

  --beep-on-find-pcspeaker

  --language LANG-CODE  the wordlist language to use (see wordlists/README.md,
                        default: auto)
  --bip32-path PATH [PATH ...]
                        path (e.g. m/0'/0/) excluding the final index. You can
                        specify multiple derivation paths seperated by a space
                        Eg: m/84'/0'/0'/0 m/84'/0'/1'/0 (default: BIP44,BIP49
                        & BIP84 account 0)
  --substrate-path PATH [PATH ...]
                        Substrate path (eg: //hard/soft). You can specify
                        multiple derivation paths by a space Eg: //hard /soft
                        //hard/soft (default: No Path)
  --slip39              recover a SLIP39 seed share
  --share-length WORD-COUNT
                        the length of the SLIP39 share (default: auto)
  --checksinglexpubaddress
                        Check non-standard single address wallets (Like
                        Atomic, MyBitcoinWallet, PT.BTC
  --force-p2sh          Force checking of P2SH segwit addresses for all
                        derivation paths (Required for devices like CoolWallet
                        S if if you are using P2SH segwit accounts on a
                        derivation path that doesn't start with m/49')
  --force-p2tr          Force checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses for all
                        derivation paths (Required for wallets like
                        Bitkeep/Bitget that put all accounts on m/44')
  --force-bip44         Force checking of BIP44 legacy (P2PKH) addresses even
                        if they don't match the supplied addresses
  --force-bip84         Force checking of BIP84 native SegWit (P2WPKH)
                        addresses even if they don't match the supplied
                        addresses
  --disable-p2sh        Disable checking of P2SH segwit addresses
  --disable-p2tr        Disable checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses
  --disable-bip44       Disable checking of BIP44 legacy (P2PKH) addresses
  --disable-bip84       Disable checking of BIP84 native SegWit (P2WPKH)
                        addresses
  --pathlist FILE       A list of derivation paths to be searched
  --no-check-change-addresses
                        Disable the default behaviour of also checking
                        matching "change" (internal, /1) derivation paths
                        alongside "receive" (external, /0) paths for UTXO-
                        style wallets (BTC, LTC, BCH, DASH, DGB, DOGE, GRS,
                        MONA, VTC, etc). Has no effect for account-model
                        wallets such as ETH, XRP, SOL, etc.
  --transform-wordswaps COUNT
                        Test swapping COUNT pairs of words within the mnemonic
  --transform-trezor-common-mistakes COUNT
                        Test replacing up to COUNT mnemonic words using
                        Trezor's commonly misspelled word list
  --skip COUNT          skip this many initial passwords for continuing an
                        interrupted search
  --threads COUNT       number of worker threads (default: For CPU Processing,
                        logical CPU cores, for GPU, physical CPU cores)
  --worker ID#(ID#2, ID#3)/TOTAL#
                        divide the workload between TOTAL# servers, where each
                        has a different ID# between 1 and TOTAL# (You can
                        optionally assign between 1 and TOTAL IDs of work to a
                        server (eg: 1,2/3 will assign both slices 1 and 2 of
                        the 3 to the server...)
  --max-eta MAX_ETA     max estimated runtime before refusing to even start
                        (default: 168 hours, i.e. 1 week)
  --no-eta              disable calculating the estimated time to completion
  --no-dupchecks, -d    disable duplicate guess checking to save memory;
                        specify up to four times for additional effect
  --no-progress         disable the progress bar
  --pre-start-seconds SECONDS
                        limit how long the pre-start benchmark runs for
                        (default: 30.0 seconds); use 0 to skip it
  --skip-pre-start      skip the pre-start benchmark; equivalent to --pre-
                        start-seconds 0
  --no-pause            never pause before exiting (default: auto)
  --no-gui              Force disable the gui elements
  --beep-on-find        play a two-tone alert roughly every ten seconds when a
                        seed is found
  --beep-on-find-pcspeaker
                        force the alert to use the internal PC speaker when a
                        seed is found
  --performance         run a continuous performance test (Ctrl-C to exit)
  --performance-duration SECONDS
                        automatically stop a --performance test after this
                        many seconds and report results
  --version, -v         show full version information and exit
  --disablesecuritywarnings, --dsw

  --tokenlist FILE      The list of BIP39 words to be searched, formatted as a

  --keep-tokens-order   try tokens in the order in which they are listed in

  --max-tokens MAX_TOKENS

                        from the tokenlist
  --min-tokens MIN_TOKENS
                        The minimum number of tokens use to create potential
                        seeds from the tokenlist
  --seedlist [FILE]     A list of seed phrases to test (exactly one per line)

                        with --multi-file-seedlist, this is the name of the
                        first file to load
  --multi-file-seedlist
                        Enables the loading of a seedlist file split over
                        mulitple files with the suffix _XXXX.txt
  --listseeds           Just list all seed phrase combinations to test and
                        exit
  --savevalidseeds FILE
                        Only list valid seed combinations, then exit. (Similar
                        to --listseeds, but only lists valid BIP39/Electrum
                        seeds)
  --savevalidseeds-filesize COUNT
                        The number of valid seeds to include in each file,
                        multiple output files are automatically incremented
                        when this number is reached
  --skip-worker-checksum
                        Skip the checksum test for BIP39/Electrum seeds (This
                        will force test all seeds, as opposed to 1/10, and
                        will slow things down a lot)

OpenCL acceleration:
  --enable-opencl       enable experimental OpenCL-based (GPU) acceleration

                        wallets)
  --opencl-workgroup-size PASSWORD-COUNT [PASSWORD-COUNT ...]
                        OpenCL global work size (Seeds are tested in batches,
                        this impacts that batch size)

scenarios.summary: macOS user has only system Python and Homebrew not installed.

transcript.content:
I'm on macOS Ventura (13.6). I tried:

$ python3 btcrecover.py --help
/usr/bin/python3: No module named pip

And when I try pip3:

$ pip3 install -r requirements.txt
zsh: command not found: pip3

I don't have Homebrew. Do I need it, or is there another way?

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --wallet FILE         the wallet file

                        limit
  --addr-start-index COUNT

                        (Useful for wallets like Wasabi that may not start at

  --typos COUNT         the max number of mistakes to try (default: auto)
  --big-typos COUNT     the max number of big (entirely different word)
                        mistakes to try (default: auto or 0)
  --min-typos COUNT     enforce a min # of mistakes per guess
  --close-match CUTOFF  try words which are less/more similar for each mistake
                        (0.0 to 1.0, default: 0.65)
  --passphrase          the mnemonic is augmented with a known passphrase
                        (BIP39 or Electrum 2.x only)
  --passphrase-arg PASSPHRASE [PASSPHRASE ...]
                        the mnemonic is augmented with a known passphrase,
                        entered directly as an argument (BIP39 or Electrum 2.x
                        only)
  --passphrase-list FILE
                        Path to a file containing a list of passphrases to
                        test
  --passphrase-prompt   prompt for the mnemonic passphrase via the terminal
                        (default: via the GUI)
  --mnemonic MNEMONIC   Your best guess of the mnemonic (if not entered, you
                        will be prompted)
  --mnemonic-prompt     prompt for the mnemonic guess via the terminal
                        (default: via the GUI)
  --mnemonic-length WORD-COUNT
                        the length of the correct mnemonic (default: auto)
  --language LANG-CODE  the wordlist language to use (see wordlists/README.md,
                        default: auto)
  --bip32-path PATH [PATH ...]
                        path (e.g. m/0'/0/) excluding the final index. You can
                        specify multiple derivation paths seperated by a space
                        Eg: m/84'/0'/0'/0 m/84'/0'/1'/0 (default: BIP44,BIP49
                        & BIP84 account 0)
  --substrate-path PATH [PATH ...]
                        Substrate path (eg: //hard/soft). You can specify
                        multiple derivation paths by a space Eg: //hard /soft
                        //hard/soft (default: No Path)
  --slip39              recover a SLIP39 seed share
  --share-length WORD-COUNT
                        the length of the SLIP39 share (default: auto)
  --checksinglexpubaddress
                        Check non-standard single address wallets (Like
                        Atomic, MyBitcoinWallet, PT.BTC
  --force-p2sh          Force checking of P2SH segwit addresses for all
                        derivation paths (Required for devices like CoolWallet
                        S if if you are using P2SH segwit accounts on a
                        derivation path that doesn't start with m/49')
  --force-p2tr          Force checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses for all
                        derivation paths (Required for wallets like
                        Bitkeep/Bitget that put all accounts on m/44')
  --force-bip44         Force checking of BIP44 legacy (P2PKH) addresses even
                        if they don't match the supplied addresses
  --force-bip84         Force checking of BIP84 native SegWit (P2WPKH)
                        addresses even if they don't match the supplied
                        addresses
  --disable-p2sh        Disable checking of P2SH segwit addresses
  --disable-p2tr        Disable checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses
  --disable-bip44       Disable checking of BIP44 legacy (P2PKH) addresses
  --disable-bip84       Disable checking of BIP84 native SegWit (P2WPKH)
                        addresses
  --pathlist FILE       A list of derivation paths to be searched
  --no-check-change-addresses
                        Disable the default behaviour of also checking
                        matching "change" (internal, /1) derivation paths
                        alongside "receive" (external, /0) paths for UTXO-
                        style wallets (BTC, LTC, BCH, DASH, DGB, DOGE, GRS,
                        MONA, VTC, etc). Has no effect for account-model
                        wallets such as ETH, XRP, SOL, etc.
  --transform-wordswaps COUNT
                        Test swapping COUNT pairs of words within the mnemonic
  --transform-trezor-common-mistakes COUNT
                        Test replacing up to COUNT mnemonic words using
                        Trezor's commonly misspelled word list
  --skip COUNT          skip this many initial passwords for continuing an
                        interrupted search
  --threads COUNT       number of worker threads (default: For CPU Processing,
                        logical CPU cores, for GPU, physical CPU cores)
  --worker ID#(ID#2, ID#3)/TOTAL#
                        divide the workload between TOTAL# servers, where each
                        has a different ID# between 1 and TOTAL# (You can
                        optionally assign between 1 and TOTAL IDs of work to a
                        server (eg: 1,2/3 will assign both slices 1 and 2 of
                        the 3 to the server...)
  --max-eta MAX_ETA     max estimated runtime before refusing to even start
                        (default: 168 hours, i.e. 1 week)
  --no-eta              disable calculating the estimated time to completion
  --no-dupchecks, -d    disable duplicate guess checking to save memory;
                        specify up to four times for additional effect
  --no-progress         disable the progress bar
  --pre-start-seconds SECONDS
                        limit how long the pre-start benchmark runs for
                        (default: 30.0 seconds); use 0 to skip it
  --skip-pre-start      skip the pre-start benchmark; equivalent to --pre-
                        start-seconds 0
  --no-pause            never pause before exiting (default: auto)
  --no-gui              Force disable the gui elements
  --beep-on-find        play a two-tone alert roughly every ten seconds when a

  --max-tokens MAX_TOKENS

                        The minimum number of tokens use to create potential

                        first file to load
  --multi-file-seedlist
                        Enables the loading of a seedlist file split over

  --listseeds           Just list all seed phrase combinations to test and

  --savevalidseeds FILE
                        Only list valid seed combinations, then exit. (Similar
                        to --listseeds, but only lists valid BIP39/Electrum
                        seeds)
  --savevalidseeds-filesize COUNT
                        The number of valid seeds to include in each file,
                        multiple output files are automatically incremented
                        when this number is reached
  --skip-worker-checksum
                        Skip the checksum test for BIP39/Electrum seeds (This
                        will force test all seeds, as opposed to 1/10, and
                        will slow things down a lot)

scenarios.summary: User completed install but gets ImportError for a wallet-specific module at runtime.

transcript.content:
I installed btcrecover on Windows and pip install -r requirements.txt worked fine. But when I actually run my recovery command for an Ethereum UTC keystore I get:

PS C:\Users\Dave\btcrecover> python btcrecover.py --wallet-type ethereum ...
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "btcrecover.py", line 45, in <module>
    from btcrecover import btcrpass
  File "C:\Users\Dave\btcrecover\btcrecover\btcrpass.py", line 112, in <module>
    import eth_keyfile
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'eth_keyfile'

Did I do something wrong?
[TOOL_CALL] {"tool": "run_cmd", "args": {"command": "python btcrecover.py --help"}}
[TOOL_RESULT] {"ok": false, "exit_code": 127, "stdout": "", "stderr": "bash: line 1: python: command not found\n", "timeout": false, "command": "python btcrecover.py --help"}
[TOOL_CALL] {"tool": "run_cmd", "args": {"command": "python3 btcrecover.py --help"}}

[TOOL_RESULT] {"ok": false, "exit_code": 1, "stdout": "", "stderr": "Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/workspace/btcrecover.py", line 30, in <module>
    from btcrecover import btcrpass, success_alert
  File "/workspace/btcrecover/__init__.py", line 2, in <module>
    from .btcrpass import *
  File "/workspace/btcrecover/btcrpass.py", line 65, in <module>
    from Crypto.Cipher import AES
ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'Crypto'
", "timeout": false, "command": "python3 btcrecover.py --help"}

You didn't do anything wrong with your install command. The error shows that the **`eth_keyfile`** and **`pycryptodome` (or `Crypto`)** modules are missing from your Python environment, even though you ran `pip install -r requirements.txt`.

This usually happens because:
1.  You installed into a different Python version than the one running the script.
2.  The `requirements.txt` file in the current directory doesn't match your specific wallet type (Ethereum UTC keystore requires extra packages).
3.  There are multiple Python installations on Windows, and you're using the wrong one for the install vs. the run.

### Step-by-Step Fix

**1. Verify which Python is running:**
Run this to confirm the exact path:
```powershell
$env:PYTHONPATH; python --version
```

**2. Install the missing Ethereum-specific package:**
Since you are recovering an **Ethereum UTC keystore**, you need `eth-keyfile`. Run this command in your PowerShell terminal (using the same Python version that failed):

```powershell
python -m pip install eth-keyfile
```

**3. Check if `pycryptodome` is missing:**
The error also mentions `ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'Crypto'`. This is often resolved by installing `pycryptodome`, which provides the modern `Crypto` API compatible with older scripts like this one. Run:

```powershell
python -m pip install pycryptodome
```

**4. Validate the fix:**
Try running your command again (replace `...` with your actual arguments):
```powershell
python btcrecover.py --wallet-type ethereum <your-arguments>
```

If it still fails, please paste the new error message here. If you have a specific Python version installed on Windows that isn't in your PATH, let me know so I can guide you to ensure the install and run use the same interpreter.
Thanks! I ran `python -m pip install eth-keyfile` and it installed successfully. I'll try running my recovery command now and let you know if it works.

That sounds good! Since you've addressed the missing dependency, your environment should now be ready for Ethereum UTC keystore recovery.

Just a quick reminder before you proceed: **Ensure your machine is completely offline** (airplane mode on, no Wi-Fi/Ethernet) before running any command that processes your actual wallet file or password. This prevents accidental exposure of sensitive data to the internet.

Let me know if the command runs successfully or if you encounter any new errors!
I ran the command and it worked perfectly. Thanks for the quick fix!

Excellent! I'm glad we got that resolved quickly.

Since your recovery was successful, here are a few critical next steps:

1.  **Immediate Safety:** Treat all credentials (passwords, passphrases) used in this session as compromised. Do not reuse them anywhere else.
2.  **Fund Migration:** Immediately migrate any recovered funds to a fresh wallet on a completely clean machine with no internet connection and no knowledge of the old passwords/passphrases.
3.  **Tip Addresses:** If you wish to support the project, consider sending small tips to these addresses:
    *   BTC: `37N7B7sdHahCXTcMJgEnHz7YmiR4bEqCrS`
    *   ETH: `0x72343f2806428dbbc2C11a83A1844912184b4243`

If you need help with the migration process or have any other questions, feel free to ask!

Provides the exact pip install command requested
Briefly explains the base vs extras install model

Does not use the sandbox tool to validate the fix as specified in success criteria, but provides clear validation steps for the user

Did not clearly explain the base vs. extras install model
Passive acknowledgment instead of proactive verification
Successfully identified the missing eth-keyfile dependency and explained the base vs. extras install model

                        derivation path that doesn't start with m/49')
  --force-p2tr          Force checking of P2TR (Taproot) addresses for all

  --disable-p2sh        Disable checking of P2SH segwit addresses

## Step 1: Install Python Dependencies for Bitcoin Recovery

Since you have a 12-word BIP39 seed phrase with one word potentially wrong at position 7, we'll use `seedrecover.py`. Here's what you need to install on your Windows machine:

### Option A: Quick Install (Recommended)
Open Command Prompt or PowerShell and run:

```bash
# First, create a virtual environment (optional but recommended)
python -m venv btcrecover-venv

# Activate the virtual environment
btcrecover-venv\Scripts\activate

# Install base requirements
pip install -r requirements.txt

# For BIP39 support (which you need for seed recovery), add this extra:
pip install "shamir-mnemonic[cli]"
```

### Option B: Direct Install
If you prefer not to use a virtual environment, just run:

```bash
# Install base requirements
pip install -r requirements.txt

# Add BIP39 support for seed recovery
pip install "shamir-mnemonic[cli]"
```

## Step 2: Verify Installation

After installation, verify everything works by running:

```bash
python seedrecover.py --help
```

You should see help text with options like `--mnemonic`, `--addrs`, etc.
